cross_connections.display_label: Named for him — born same year Brainerd died, 1747

naming_inspiration.description: Named for the Connecticut missionary David Brainerd (X02351, 1718-1747), who died October 9, 1747 — just seven months after Brainard Hooker's birth. Brainerd was Connecticut-born (Haddam) and celebrated for his missionary work when the Hooker baby was named.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War soldier; led family north to Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Brainard Hooker served in the Revolutionary War and moved his family from Wethersfield, Connecticut to Middlebury, Vermont, where he died in October 1808 at age sixty-one. He was survived by his wife Mary Deming Hooker, who outlived him by thirty-five years, dying in 1843 at age ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in 1747 when David Brainerd also died
narrative_blocks.body: Brainard Hooker was born in Woodbury, Connecticut on March 4, 1747 — the same year that the renowned Presbyterian missionary David Brainerd died in Northampton, Massachusetts on October 9, 1747. The name 'Brainard' was rare in colonial Connecticut, but Brainerd was Connecticut-born (Haddam) and celebrated across New England for his missionary work among Delaware Indians; his parents almost certainly named the baby in honor of the dying missionary.

burial.plot_notes: Case Street Cemetery, Middlebury VT per FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.header: Married Abel Case on his birthday — September 22
narrative_blocks.body: Jennette Hooker married Abel Case on September 22, 1822 — Abel's 20th birthday, since he was born on September 22, 1802 — a coincidence mirrored by her sister Sarah, who six years later married on her own 20th birthday on March 11, 1828. The Case family moved from Middlebury, Vermont to Carmel, Michigan, and Jennette died at Charlotte, Michigan in 1873.

narrative_blocks.header: Married on her twentieth birthday, March 11, 1828
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker was born on March 11, 1808 in Middlebury, Vermont, and married Nelson Mead on that exact date twenty years later — her twentieth birthday — mirroring her sister Jennette, who in 1822 had married Abel Case on Abel's own 20th birthday. Sarah and Nelson moved west to Charlotte, Michigan, where she died in 1869 and he outlived her by twenty-seven years, dying in 1896.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

narrative_blocks.body: Emeline Hooker married Stephen Gorham Mead on April 6, 1831 — the younger brother of Nelson Mead, who three years earlier had married her sister Sarah Hooker. Both pairs were sons and daughters of Middlebury, Vermont families whose lives intertwined through two simultaneous Hooker-Mead alliances before both couples moved west to Michigan.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Victory Union Cemetery Victory NY

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Polly died in New York City
narrative_blocks.body: Calvin Hooker's wife Polly Kenyon Hooker died on March 10, 1835 in New York City — far from their home in Cayuga County — leaving Calvin a widower for twenty-four years until his own death in Victory, New York in 1859. Calvin outlived two siblings who also died in 1812: his sister Louisa, dead twenty-four days after her wedding, and his brother Harris, who died a prisoner of war at Quebec.

death.cause: Died prisoner of war, War of 1812

military_service.notes: Captured. Died prisoner of war at Quebec Canada.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 prisoner; died at Quebec
narrative_blocks.body: Harris Hooker served as a soldier in the War of 1812 and was captured by British forces, dying as a prisoner of war in Quebec, Canada. His fate closely parallels that of Ezra Griswold Stiles — another Hooker descendant who was also captured in the War of 1812, documented in the story `ezra_griswold_stiles_war_of_1812_captivity`.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

burial.plot_notes: Case Street Cemetery Middlebury VT per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Married September 12; died twenty-four days later
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa Hooker married Burnham Phelps on September 12, 1812, and died just twenty-four days later on October 6, 1812 — at twenty-one, in the same year her father Brainard died and her brother Harris died a prisoner of war at Quebec. Her husband married her sister Mary Deming Hooker fourteen months later, who gave him the family the brief first marriage could not.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

burial.plot_notes: Pioneer Cemetery Dover Bureau County IL

narrative_blocks.header: Two marriages, two Hooker sisters, fourteen months apart
narrative_blocks.body: Burnham Phelps married Louisa Hooker on September 12, 1812; she died just twenty-four days later on October 6, 1812. He married her sister Mary Deming Hooker on January 7, 1814, and together they raised eight children — including twins born July 7, 1823 named Christopher Columbus and Americus Vespusius.

sources.label: FindAGrave memorial records

narrative_blocks.header: Married her late sister's husband; bore explorer-named twins
narrative_blocks.body: Fourteen months after her sister Louisa's death — just twenty-four days into Louisa's marriage to Burnham Phelps — Mary Deming Hooker married Burnham herself on January 7, 1814, the practice of marrying a deceased wife's sister recognized in New England as a form of family continuity. Among their eight children were twins born July 7, 1823 named Christopher Columbus and Americus Vespusius — honoring both Atlantic explorers in a single act of naming, though Americus died at age three in 1826.

narrative_blocks.header: Died childless; her sister Diantha married her widower
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Strong Phelps died on February 22, 1866 at age forty-five — without children — and just eight months later her sister Diantha married her widower Franklin Hope, repeating the very pattern that had brought their own parents together: their mother Mary Deming Hooker had married Burnham Phelps after Burnham's first wife Louisa Hooker died in 1812. The Hooker-Phelps family repeated this pattern across two generations.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Berean Cemetery Cherry Bureau County IL

naming_inspiration.description: Named for Christopher Columbus, the explorer who reached the Americas in 1492.

narrative_blocks.body: Christopher Columbus Phelps was born as a twin with Americus Vespucius Phelps in 1823 — both named for the explorers most closely associated with the Americas: Christopher Columbus, who first reached the New World, and Amerigo Vespucci, whose name became America itself. Americus Vespucius died in 1826 at age three; Christopher went on to settle in Bureau County, Illinois and have two children who would leave documented descendants into the twenty-first century.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave memorial records
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Named Americus Vespusius; died in Middlebury aged three
narrative_blocks.body: Americus Vespusius Phelps was born on July 7, 1823, the twin of Christopher Columbus Phelps — his parents naming both boys for the explorers whose voyages mapped the Atlantic world. He died in Middlebury on September 12, 1826, at age three.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

narrative_blocks.header: Like her mother, married her dead sister's widower
narrative_blocks.body: Diantha Phelps married Franklin Hope on October 30, 1866 — just eight months after his first wife, her sister Lucy Strong Phelps Hope (HD4447), had died in Westfield, Illinois in February 1866. The pattern precisely mirrors that of Diantha's own mother, Mary Deming Hooker (H01253), who in January 1814 married Burnham Phelps, widower of her sister Louisa Hooker — the same generational echo, separated by fifty-two years.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

bio.notes: Also spelled Curtiss in some records
